parentQuestion: Which of the following statements about connective tissue is FALSE?

scaffolds.q: What is a defining feature of connective tissue?
options: Cells tightly packed with no matrix
Abundant extracellular matrix with scattered cells
scaffolds.explain: Connective tissue has ABUNDANT EXTRACELLULAR MATRIX with SCATTERED CELLS. This is the opposite of epithelium, which has tightly packed cells with minimal matrix.

wrongExplains.0: Tightly packed cells describes epithelial tissue.

scaffolds.explain: VASCULAR means HAVING BLOOD VESSELS. 'A-vascular' means WITHOUT blood vessels (the 'a-' prefix means 'not' or 'without').

scaffolds.q: Are most connective tissues vascular or avascular?
scaffolds.explain: MOST connective tissues are VASCULAR — bone, fat, and loose connective tissues all have blood vessels. The major exception is cartilage, which is avascular.

wrongExplains.0: Most connective tissues have blood supply.
wrongExplains.3: Many connective tissues besides bone are vascular.

scaffolds.q: Which connective tissue is the exception — lacking blood vessels?
scaffolds.explain: CARTILAGE is AVASCULAR — it has NO BLOOD VESSELS. Nutrients reach cartilage cells (chondrocytes) only by slow diffusion through the matrix.

wrongExplains.0: Bone is highly vascular with an excellent blood supply.
wrongExplains.2: Blood IS the vascular system itself.
wrongExplains.3: Adipose tissue has a good blood supply.

options: It lacks blood vessels, so healing supplies arrive slowly by diffusion
scaffolds.explain: Cartilage LACKS BLOOD VESSELS, so healing supplies (nutrients, immune cells, growth factors) arrive slowly by DIFFUSION. No express delivery means slow repair.

wrongExplains.0: Cartilage actually has relatively few cells.
wrongExplains.2: Hardness doesn't directly affect healing rate.

scaffolds.q: What embryonic tissue do all connective tissues develop from?
scaffolds.explain: All connective tissues develop from MESENCHYME, which comes from MESODERM. This common origin unifies the diverse connective tissue family.

wrongExplains.0: Ectoderm forms skin and nervous system.
wrongExplains.3: Neural crest forms some connective tissues but not all.

scaffolds.q: What are the main functions of connective tissue?
options: Binding, support, protection, insulation, and transport
scaffolds.explain: Connective tissue provides BINDING (ligaments), SUPPORT (bone, cartilage), PROTECTION (bone, fat padding), INSULATION (fat), and TRANSPORT (blood).

scaffolds.q: Why is 'connective tissue is highly vascular' a false statement?
options: No connective tissue has blood vessels
Cartilage is avascular, disproving the general statement
All connective tissues are equally vascular
scaffolds.explain: CARTILAGE is AVASCULAR, disproving the statement that 'connective tissue is highly vascular.' Most are vascular, but cartilage is the notable exception.

wrongExplains.0: Most connective tissues do have blood vessels.
wrongExplains.2: Blood IS classified as connective tissue.

scaffolds.q: Which statement about connective tissue is FALSE?
options: All connective tissue is highly vascular
scaffolds.explain: 'All connective tissue is highly vascular' is FALSE because CARTILAGE is AVASCULAR. The other statements (abundant matrix, mesenchyme origin, support function) are true.

wrongExplains.0: Abundant ECM IS a true characteristic of connective tissue.
wrongExplains.2: Developing from mesenchyme IS true for all connective tissues.
wrongExplains.3: Support and binding ARE connective tissue functions.
